text: All plants inevitably absorb Na⁺ when exposed to saline environments. Once it enters the symplast, Na⁺ is partitioned both within individual cells and across various tissues and organs. While this accumulation can assist in lowering cellular water potential, it also carries the risk of severe toxicity. Consequently, the precise control of Na⁺ fluxes is essential for survival. Increasing evidence suggests that the distinction between salt-tolerant and salt-sensitive species does not stem from different types of transporters, but rather from how effectively they regulate uptake and internal distribution. Recent research has identified a diverse array of regulatory mechanisms involving calcium signaling, cyclic nucleotides, reactive oxygen species, and hormonal pathways, as well as complex transcriptional and post-translational modifications. This review provides a comprehensive overview of intra- and intercellular Na⁺ movement and summarizes our current understanding of how these fluxes are orchestrated during the initial stages of salt stress.
